perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT (this "Agreement") is made effective as of ______________ --------- between INTERTRUST TECHNOLOGIES CORPORATION ("InterTrust"), a Delaware ---------- corporation, having a place of business at 460 Oakmead Parkway, Sunnyvale, California 90486, and ___________("Recipient"), an individual citizen of --------- __________________________, residing at ___________________________________________________________, and an employee of National Westminster Bank Plc ("NatWest"), a United Kingdom corporation with ------- offices at 41 Lothbury, London, England.
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT (this "Agreement") is made effective as of ______________ --------- between INTERTRUST TECHNOLOGIES CORPORATION ("InterTrust"), a Delaware ---------- corporation, having a place of business at 460 Oakmead Parkway, Sunnyvale, California 90486, and ___________("Recipient"), an individual citizen of --------- __________________________, residing at ___________________________________________________________, and an employee of National Westminster Bank Plc ("NatWest"), a United Kingdom corporation with ------- offices at 41 Lothbury, London, England. The Recipient will not be eligible for whistleblower protection under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that prevents the recipient from being eligible for whistleblower protection contradicts federal law if the recipient discloses information related to a violation of law. The contract's restriction on whistleblower eligibility is an unenforceable provision.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1514A - Civil action to protect against retaliation in fraud cases
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1514A protects employees of publicly traded companies from retaliation for providing information about conduct that the employee reasonably believes constitutes a violation of the securities laws. The modified text, in barring the recipient from whistleblower protection, directly contradicts this federal protection against retaliation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text adds a clause denying the recipient whistleblower protection, which directly contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1514A. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 18 USC 1514A provides "Whistleblower Protection for Employees of Publicly Traded Companies". The snippet specifically mentions protection from retaliation, which the added clause in the modified text aims to circumvent. This creates a direct conflict with the federal law, as it attempts to remove a protection explicitly granted by law. Therefore, the contradiction is clear and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the United States of America and the Commonwealth of Virginia, USA, without reference to conflicts of laws principles. All disputes arising out of or relating to the subject matter of this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, Alexandria Division Virginia, or if jurisdiction does not properly lie in such court, the Commonwealth courts in Alexandria, Virginia.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the United States of America and the Commonwealth of Virginia, USA, without reference to conflicts of laws principles. All disputes arising out of or relating to the subject matter of this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the courts of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing all disputes to be settled in China would not be legally enforceable in many circumstances, as it attempts to circumvent US jurisdiction and potentially deprive a US company of its legal rights. This clause would be deemed unconscionable and against public policy in many US courts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: This law grants federal courts jurisdiction over disputes between citizens of different states or between citizens of a state and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, provided the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000. By mandating that all disputes be resolved in the courts of the People's Republic of China, the modified clause attempts to strip US federal courts of this jurisdiction, potentially violating 28 U.S. Code § 1332 and undermining the principle of diversity jurisdiction.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: diversity jurisdiction | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you diversity jurisdiction Diversity jurisdiction refers to one way a federal court can obtain subject matter jurisdiction over a given case (the other method being federal question jurisdiction ). Diversity jurisdiction is codified in Title 28 , Section 1332 of the United States Code (28 U.S.C. § 1332(a)) . For a court to exercise diversity jurisdiction, the amount in controversy must exceed $75,000 and complete diversity of citizenship must exist. Complete diversity of citizenship occurs when no plaintiff and defendant are domiciled in the same state. In determining whether diversity jurisdiction exists, a corporation is considered to be a citizen of both its state of incorporation and its principal place of business . Diversity jurisdiction is somewhat modified in class action lawsuits. Specifically, the Class Action Fairness Act of 2005 (CAFA) modified the complete diversity requirement. For a federal court to obtain subject-matter jurisdiction over a class action, the parties need only to satisfy minimal diversity. Minimal diversity occurs when at least one plaintiff is a resident from a state that is different from at least one defendant. Because diversity jurisdiction is a form of concurrent jurisdiction , parties can choose to bring their case in state court even if the requirements for diversity jurisdiction are met. That said, if diversity jurisdiction is available, a defendant in a state court case can unilaterally choose to move the case to federal court through the process of removal . A court exercising diversity jurisdiction is subject to the Erie doctrine , which states that federal courts must follow the substantive law of the state in which they reside. This doctrine limits the ability of plaintiffs and defendants to obtain an advantage through the use/decision not to use diversity jurisdiction. The Erie doctrine takes its name from the seminal case Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins . [Last reviewed in September of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords civil procedure Federal Rules of Civil Procedure diversity jurisdiction subject matter jurisdiction Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts judicial administration wex definitions civil procedure courts and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates that all disputes be resolved in the courts of the People's Republic of China, which directly contradicts the diversity jurisdiction granted to US federal courts under 28 U.S.C. § 1332. This statute, as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants district courts original jurisdiction over civil actions exceeding $75,000 between citizens of different states or between a citizen of a state and citizens of a foreign state. The change effectively strips US federal courts of this jurisdiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines diversity jurisdiction and references 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a), further supporting the existence of this jurisdiction. By forcing all disputes to be settled in China, the modified clause attempts to circumvent this US jurisdiction and undermine the principle of diversity jurisdiction, creating a clear legal contradiction.
